content: Compound given below will produce effervescence when mixed with aqueous sodium bicarbonate solution
explanation:
Acid having lower $\mathrm{p} K_a$ are soluble in $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$.

$$ \therefore \mathrm{p} K_a(\text { Picric acid })<\mathrm{pK}_a \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 $$

Thus, picric acid is soluble in $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$.

What is the correct order of acidity of salicylic acid, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, and 2, 6-dihydroxybenzoic acid ?
explanation:
The acidic strength of an acid increases with the increase in stability of its conjugate base by intramolecular hydrogen bonding.

(i) Salicylic acid

(ii) 2, 6-dihydroxybenzoic acid

Also, pKa value of 2, 6-dihydroxybenzoic acid is greater than salicylic acid.

$$\therefore$$ It is a stronger acid.

(iii) In 4-hydroxygenzoic acid $$-$$OH group show +R effect.

Thus, it decreases the acidic character 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.

$$\therefore$$ The acidity order is 2, 6-dihydroxybenzoic acid > salicylic acid > 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.

content: The exact order of acidity of the compounds p-nitrophenol, acetic acid, acetylene and ethanol is
explanation:
The correct order of acidity of the given compounds is acetylene < ethanol < p-nitrophenol < acetic acid.

Ethanol is stronger acid than acetylene. This is because after the loss of acidic proton (H+) the corresponding anion formed by ethanol is stabilised by the electronegative oxygen atom. Now p-nitrophenol is more acidic than ethanol because the phenoxide ion obtained after the deprotonation is stabilised by resonance effect and also by EWG effect (which is caused by $$-$$NO2 group).

Likewise, acetic acid is more acids than all of the given compounds as the formed carboxylate ion is stabilised by resonance which is due to the presence of electronegative oxygen atom.

content: For the above three esters, the order of rates of alkaline hydrolysis is
explanation:
Alkaline hydrolysis of an ester (carboxylic acid derivative) follows acyl SN2 mechanism. It is as follows :


Rate of SN2 mechanism depends on the polar nature of $$\rangle C = O$$ group of $$ - $$ COOR group. Electron withdrawing group ($$ - $$ R>I) increases the rate of SN2 as it promotes the nucleophilic attack whereas electron donating group (+ R > + I) decreases the rate of SN2 reaction as it demotes the nucleophilic attack.

Here, the nature and order of functional groups attached para to benzene ring are :

$$\mathop { - N{O_2}(I)}\limits_{( - R)}^{} $$$$ > - \mathop {Me(III)}\limits_{( + I)}^{} > - $$ $$\mathop {OMe(III)}\limits_{( + R)}^{} $$

So, the order of rate of alkaline hydrolysis is I > III > II

content: The conversion(s) that can be carried out by bromine in carbon tetrachloride solvent is/are
explanation:
$$\mathrm{PhCH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_3 \xrightarrow[\mathrm{CCl}_4]{\mathrm{Br}_2} \mathrm{PhCHBrCHBr \textrm {CH } _ { 3 }}$$

In this reaction addition of $$\mathrm{Br}_2$$ takes place in presence of $$\mathrm{CCl}_4$$ solvent.

$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Br}_2]{\mathrm{RedP}} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHBrCOOH}$$

The above reaction is a Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction and does not use $$\mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{CCl}_4$$.

The above reaction is a borodine Hunsdiecker reaction, which uses $$\mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{CCl}_4$$.

Thus, options given in $$(a, d)$$ uses $$\mathrm{Br}_2$$ in $$\mathrm{CCl}_4$$.
